1: ʾim-yhwh lōʾ-yibneh bayit šāwĕʾ ʿāmĕlû bônāyw bô ʾim-yhwh lōʾ-yišmor-ʿîr šāwĕʾ šāqad šômēr — "unless the LORD builds the house, the builders labor in vain; unless the LORD guards the city, the sentinel watches for nothing" — šāwĕʾ (vanity, emptiness, futility, falsehood) — the same word used in the third commandment ("take the name of the LORD in vain," Exodus 20:7). The attribution to Solomon (lĕšĕlōmōh) gives the verse a Solomon-temple resonance: the very temple Solomon built was only effective because YHWH chose to dwell in it (1 Kings 8:27). bayit (house) has double force: the physical temple AND the Davidic dynasty ("your house shall be made sure," 2 Samuel 7:11,16).
2: šāwĕʾ lākem maškîmê qûm mĕʾaḥărê šebet ʾōkĕlê leḥem hāʾăṣābîm kēn yittēn lîdîdô šēnāʾ — "it is pointless for you to rise early, stay up late, eating the bread of anxious toil — for he gives sleep to those he loves" — maškîmê qûm (those who rise early, from šāḵam, to be early, start at dawn) — the pre-dawn laborer. leḥem hāʾăṣābîm (bread of sorrows/pain/anxious toil) — ʿăṣab (pain, toil, sorrow) — the curse vocabulary of Genesis 3:16-17 (toil, pain, eating by sweat). kēn yittēn lîdîdô šēnāʾ — "for so he gives to his beloved sleep" — dîdô (his beloved, from yādîd, beloved, dear) — the beloved of YHWH. Sleep is not earned by exhaustion but given as a gift; the one who rests in YHWH's provision does not need to anxiously secure their own existence through sleepless toil.
3: hinnēh naḥălat yhwh bānîm śākār pĕrî habbāṭen — "Yes, children are an inheritance from the LORD; they are a reward he gives" — naḥălat yhwh (the inheritance/heritage of the LORD) — children are not merely the product of human biology but a divine gift, a share in what the LORD distributes. śākār (reward, wages) — normally used for labor payment; here applied to the fruit of the womb, suggesting that children are the "wages" YHWH gives for covenant faithfulness rather than for anxious toil (cf. v2's bread of anxious toil → v3's inherited children).
4: kĕḥiṣṣîm bĕyad-gibbôr kēn bĕnê hannĕʿûrîm — "children born in a man's prime are like arrows in the hand of a warrior" — ḥiṣṣîm (arrows) — the plural of ḥēṣ (arrow, from ḥāṣaṣ, to cut). bĕnê hannĕʿûrîm (sons of youth/the prime years, lit. "sons of the youthful ones") — children born when the father is young enough that they will be fully grown when he is old, providing protection in his vulnerability. The arrow metaphor: straight, well-prepared, directed with force. Arrows in the hand of a warrior are ready for deployment — these children are the father's defense in the vulnerable years.
5: ʾašrê haggeber ʾăšer millēʾ ʾet-ašpātô mēhem lōʾ-yēbōšû kî-yĕdabbĕrû ʾet-ʾôyĕbîm baššāʿar — "blessed is the man who has his quiver full of them! He will not be put to shame when he confronts his enemies at the gate" — ʾašpāh (quiver, arrow-case) — the image completes the arrow metaphor. baššāʿar (at the gate) — the city gate was the judicial arena where legal disputes were settled; the man with grown sons standing beside him would have moral weight in legal proceedings and practical protection against false accusations.
